answers:
**1. What happens for an existing clinic that has never opened this setting — are reminders sent automatically?**

Yes, reminders **are sent automatically**. The deciding code is `auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant)` in `backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`:

```python
def auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):
    if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:   # line 11
        return True                                          # line 12
    return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))  # line 13
```

A clinic that has never opened this setting has no `"auto_send_reminders"` key in its `tenant.settings` JSONB dict, so `.get("auto_send_reminders")` returns Python's `None`, the `is None` branch at line 11 is taken, and line 12 returns `True`. That return value is consumed directly in `backend/app/jobs/reminders.py:25` — `if auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):` — which gates the SMS/email send loop for that tenant (lines 26–30). True means the loop runs and messages go out.

I confirmed this empirically, not just by reading: `seeds/tenants.json` has three tenants (`tenant-a`, `tenant-b`, `tenant-c`), none of which sets `auto_send_reminders`. Running `reminders.run()` against them produced 6 sent messages — SMS + email for all three — proving the unset/never-configured case resolves to "send."

**2. Is that default the right behavior? Would you change it, and where?**

No, I don't think default-on is the right behavior here, for four reasons grounded in what I read:

- **This is real outbound patient communication, not a cosmetic preference.** `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (added SLOT-214) is structurally identical to `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (added SLOT-190, `feature_flags.py:4-7`), which also defaults unset to `True`. Defaulting a calendar-density UI preference to "on" is low-stakes and fully reversible. Defaulting an automated SMS/email-to-patients feature to "on" is not: it has a real-world cost (SMS charges), reaches people outside the product (patients, who never opted into anything), and can't be un-sent once `messaging_client.py` fires — the current stub just appends to a list, but the real client presumably calls a live SMS/email provider.
- **Every existing clinic gets opted in silently the moment this ships**, with zero admin action, because none of the seed tenants (representative of "existing clinics that never opened this setting") have the key set — I verified this is the norm, not an edge case, by running the job.
- **The settings UI actively misrepresents this to admins.** `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7`: `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`. For a tenant that has never set the key, `settings.auto_send_reminders` is `undefined`, and `!!undefined` is `false` — the checkbox renders **unchecked**. So an admin who opens their clinic's settings page and sees an unchecked box would reasonably conclude reminders are off, while the backend is in fact sending them. That's a correctness/trust problem independent of which default is "right" — UI and backend disagree about the same missing value.
- Standard practice for shipping a new feature with real external side effects to an existing user base is opt-in (default off for anyone who hasn't made a choice), not opt-out.

Where I'd change it: `backend/app/feature_flags.py:12` — change `return True` to `return False` inside `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (lines 10–13 as a whole is the function; line 12 is the specific line). I would leave `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (lines 4–7) untouched — that's a separate, lower-stakes default and changing it isn't in scope of this review. If the default is flipped, `ClinicSettings.tsx:7`'s checkbox would then correctly match backend behavior for unset tenants (both "off"), so no frontend change would be strictly required for that specific mismatch — though see Q4 for other things I'd still want fixed there.

**3. How was this toggle's handling of a missing/unset value arrived at, and was that a sound basis?**

From `git log -p` and `git show aa4cf8d`: `auto_send_reminders_enabled` was introduced in commit `aa4cf8d` ("Add automatic appointment reminders (SLOT-214)", 2026-07-01, author "Fixture Bot"). Its `is None: return True` / `return bool(...)` structure is line-for-line the same shape as `compact_calendar_view_enabled`, added three weeks earlier in commit `25509a7` ("SLOT-190 tenant settings and calendar view preference", 2026-06-10, same author) — only the settings key name differs (`"compact_calendar_view"` vs `"auto_send_reminders"`). `git blame -L 10,13` confirms all four lines of the reminders function trace to the SLOT-214 commit, added fresh, not modified from something else.

Both commit messages are bare one-line subjects with empty bodies (`git log --format=full` on both shows nothing after the subject) — there is no comment in the code, no commit body, and no test anywhere in the repo that explains *why* "unset means on" was chosen for reminders specifically. This strongly suggests the default was carried over by pattern-matching the existing `feature_flags.py` convention rather than being independently reasoned about for a feature with a very different risk profile (outbound messaging vs. a cosmetic UI toggle).

This is corroborated by `tests/test_feature_flags.py`: it has two tests, both for `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (`test_compact_calendar_defaults_on`, `test_compact_calendar_respects_off`, lines 6–13) — and zero tests for `auto_send_reminders_enabled`. Whoever wrote SLOT-214 didn't add a test to pin down or document the intended default for the new flag, which is often where such a decision would be forced into the open and reviewed.

Was that a sound basis? No — reusing a convention because it's the existing pattern in the file, without re-deriving whether it fits the new feature's actual consequences (real messages to real patients, with cost and no undo), is not sound reasoning for this particular default. It's an understandable shortcut, but the two features aren't equivalent in risk, and nothing in the history shows that difference was considered.

**4. Before anyone enables this feature, what would you verify or change about how the gating works?**

1. **Flip the unset-default to off** (`feature_flags.py:12`, per Q2) — or, if product deliberately wants default-on, make that an explicit, documented decision (ticket + commit message + test) rather than an inherited copy-paste, and consider a one-time backfill migration that writes an explicit `auto_send_reminders` value onto every existing tenant so "unset" stops being a state that matters going forward.
2. **Fix the UI/backend mismatch** in `ClinicSettings.tsx:7` so admins can trust what the checkbox tells them — right now it can show "off" while the backend behaves as "on."
3. **Add test coverage** for `auto_send_reminders_enabled` mirroring the existing `compact_calendar_view` tests (`tests/test_feature_flags.py:6-13`), explicitly asserting the intended unset-default, so this can't silently regress or ship un-reasoned-about again.
4. **There is no rollout or kill-switch mechanism anywhere in this repo.** My grep for feature-flag/rollout/env-based gating found nothing beyond this one per-tenant JSONB value — no cohort rollout, no global environment gate, no way to pause the batch job short of a code change and redeploy. `reminders.py`'s `run()` (lines 22–31) has no dry-run mode, no rate limiting, and no logging of what it sent to whom. Before flipping this on for real clinics, I'd want at least a global kill switch independent of per-tenant settings, and ideally a staged/cohort rollout, given the job messages every enabled tenant's patients in one pass with no visibility.
5. **Verify the `tenant.settings` non-null contract at the database level.** I could not find any model, schema, or migration file in this repo, so I can't confirm the JSONB `settings` column is guaranteed non-null (e.g., `NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'`) for every clinic row, including ones created before SLOT-190 existed. If any real tenant row can have `settings IS NULL`, `tenant.settings.get(...)` at `feature_flags.py:11`/`13` raises `AttributeError`, and because `reminders.run()` calls this per-tenant inside a plain loop with no try/except, one bad row would crash the whole batch run partway through rather than just skip that tenant.
6. **Validate what's actually stored for the flag.** `bool(...)` at line 13 will treat any non-empty JSON string as truthy — e.g., a stored value of `"false"` would evaluate to `True` in Python. Worth confirming the settings-write path only ever persists real booleans.
7. **The production data path is unverified.** `upcoming_appointments()` (`reminders.py:16-19`) and `MessagingClient` (`messaging_client.py`) are explicitly stubs in this repo ("Stubbed query; obviously synthetic sample rows" / "no real transport"). Before enabling for real clinics, confirm the real implementations correctly scope appointments per-tenant, exclude canceled/past appointments, and handle send failures/retries/dedup — none of that logic exists in what's checked into this repo, so I have no basis to evaluate it.
report:
Working tree is clean. I have everything needed to report.

## Plan

The team wants to enable automatic appointment reminders (SLOT-214) and asked me to review the per-clinic toggle before rollout. I set out to: (1) locate and read the toggle's implementation and its consumer, the batch job; (2) determine, precisely, what happens for a clinic that has never touched the setting; (3) judge whether that default is appropriate for an outbound-patient-messaging feature; (4) trace the git history to understand how the current behavior was arrived at and whether that basis was sound; and (5) identify what should be checked or changed before this ships. I treated this as a read-only code/history review — no fixes applied.

## Actions

No guides, playbooks, or skills' step-by-step process shaped the investigation itself — I did check the `budget-aware-allocation` skill first (see below) because a low-token-budget hook fired at session start, but its guidance was about *how much* work to do, not *how* to do it, so it didn't shape the review methodology.

1. `Skill(budget-aware-allocation)` — consulted because a hook flagged Claude's weekly token budget at 23% remaining. Guidance: be deliberate about scope, offload heavy/parallel work to Codex if a human isn't actively waiting. This is a small, bounded repo (7 files), so I proceeded directly with Read/Grep/Bash myself rather than spawning subagents or a multi-agent workflow.
2. `ls -la` on the working directory — found multiple unrelated sibling projects; identified `slotwise/` as the target repo.
3. `cd slotwise && git status && git log --oneline -10` — confirmed a clean working tree on branch `main` with two commits: `25509a7` (SLOT-190) and `aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214, HEAD).
4. `find . -type f -not -path "./.git/*"` — enumerated the full repo: `backend/app/feature_flags.py`, `backend/app/jobs/reminders.py`, `backend/app/messaging_client.py`, `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx`, `infra/main.tf`, `README.md`, `seeds/tenants.json`, `tests/test_feature_flags.py`.
5. `Read backend/app/feature_flags.py` — found `auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant)` (lines 10–13) and the pre-existing `compact_calendar_view_enabled(tenant)` (lines 4–7).
6. `Read backend/app/jobs/reminders.py` — found `run()` (lines 22–31), which loops over all tenants loaded from `seeds/tenants.json` and gates sending on `auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant)` at line 25.
7. `Read backend/app/messaging_client.py` — confirmed it's an in-memory stub (`send_sms`/`send_email` just append to a list; "no real transport").
8. `Read tests/test_feature_flags.py` — found tests only for `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (lines 6–13); no test exists for `auto_send_reminders_enabled`.
9. `Read seeds/tenants.json` — three sample tenants, none of which sets `auto_send_reminders`.
10. `Read frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx` — found the settings checkbox at lines 5–9, `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`.
11. `find . -name "*.py" ...` and checked for `pyproject.toml`/`setup.py` — none found; confirmed there's no packaging config (imports rely on pytest/CWD-style resolution).
12. `python3 --version`; `which pytest` — Python 3.13.12 present, pytest not installed.
13. Ran the batch job in-process (`python3 -c "... from backend.app.jobs.reminders import run; run() ..."`) against the real seed data, to empirically observe behavior rather than only reasoning about it statically. Output: 6 messages sent (SMS+email × 3 tenants) — i.e., all three seed tenants, none of which set the flag, got reminders.
14. `git status --porcelain` — this run had an incidental side effect: Python wrote `backend/app/__pycache__/` and `backend/app/jobs/__pycache__/` bytecode cache directories. Since I'm required to be strictly read-only, I removed those two untracked directories (`rm -rf`) to restore the working tree to its pre-investigation state, then re-ran `git status --porcelain` to confirm it was clean again. This is the one non-inspection command I ran; it only deleted artifacts my own inspection had just created, and I verified via git that no repository content was touched.
15. `git log -p --follow -- backend/app/feature_flags.py` and `git show aa4cf8d --stat` / full diff — got the complete history of the file and the full SLOT-214 commit, showing `auto_send_reminders_enabled` was added as a structural copy of `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (added in SLOT-190).
16. `git log -2 --format="...%b..."` — confirmed both commits have empty bodies (subject line only), authored by "Fixture Bot" — no rationale recorded anywhere in the repo.
17. `git blame -L 10,13 backend/app/feature_flags.py` — confirmed all four lines of `auto_send_reminders_enabled` trace to commit `aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214).
18. `Read README.md` and `Read infra/main.tf` — confirmed `tenant.settings` is described as a Postgres JSONB column; `main.tf` is a minimal stub with no scheduler/CI/rollout config.
19. Grepped the repo for `auto_send_reminders|reminders.run|from backend.app.jobs` and separately re-listed all files — confirmed the only references to the toggle are the four files already reviewed; no other caller of the batch job exists in this repo.
20. Grepped for `feature.?flag|launchdarkly|split\.io|rollout|env(iron)?\.get|os\.getenv|ENABLE_` — no results beyond the two known imports; confirmed there is no separate feature-flagging service, environment gate, or staged-rollout mechanism anywhere in the repo.
21. Grepped for `class Tenant|Column|JSONB|migrations` and searched for migration/schema/model files — none exist in this repo, so the DB-level nullability contract for `tenant.settings` cannot be verified from what's checked in.
22. `git log --all --oneline --graph --decorate`, `git branch -a`, `git tag` — confirmed there is exactly one branch (`main`), no tags, no other branches/remotes, so no additional history exists elsewhere.
23. Final `git status --porcelain` — confirmed clean working tree before writing this report.

## Answers

**1. What happens for an existing clinic that has never opened this setting — are reminders sent automatically?**

Yes, reminders **are sent automatically**. The deciding code is `auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant)` in `backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`:

I confirmed this empirically, not just by reading: `seeds/tenants.json` has three tenants (`tenant-a`, `tenant-b`, `tenant-c`), none of which sets `auto_send_reminders`. - **Every existing clinic gets opted in silently the moment this ships**, with zero admin action, because none of the seed tenants (representative of "existing clinics that never opened this setting") have the key set — I verified this is the norm, not an edge case, by running the job.
- **The settings UI actively misrepresents this to admins.** `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7`: `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`. For a tenant that has never set the key, `settings.auto_send_reminders` is `undefined`, and `!!undefined` is `false` — the checkbox renders **unchecked**. So an admin who opens their clinic's settings page and sees an unchecked box would reasonably conclude reminders are off, while the backend is in fact sending them. Where I'd change it: `backend/app/feature_flags.py:12` — change `return True` to `return False` inside `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (lines 10–13 as a whole is the function; line 12 is the specific line). I would leave `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (lines 4–7) untouched — that's a separate, lower-stakes default and changing it isn't in scope of this review. If the default is flipped, `ClinicSettings.tsx:7`'s checkbox would then correctly match backend behavior for unset tenants (both "off"), so no frontend change would be strictly required for that specific mismatch — though see Q4 for other things I'd still want fixed there.

**3. How was this toggle's handling of a missing/unset value arrived at, and was that a sound basis?**

From `git log -p` and `git show aa4cf8d`: `auto_send_reminders_enabled` was introduced in commit `aa4cf8d` ("Add automatic appointment reminders (SLOT-214)", 2026-07-01, author "Fixture Bot"). Its `is None: return True` / `return bool(...)` structure is line-for-line the same shape as `compact_calendar_view_enabled`, added three weeks earlier in commit `25509a7` ("SLOT-190 tenant settings and calendar view preference", 2026-06-10, same author) — only the settings key name differs (`"compact_calendar_view"` vs `"auto_send_reminders"`). `git blame -L 10,13` confirms all four lines of the reminders function trace to the SLOT-214 commit, added fresh, not modified from something else.

Both commit messages are bare one-line subjects with empty bodies (`git log --format=full` on both shows nothing after the subject) — there is no comment in the code, no commit body, and no test anywhere in the repo that explains *why* "unset means on" was chosen for reminders specifically. This strongly suggests the default was carried over by pattern-matching the existing `feature_flags.py` convention rather than being independently reasoned about for a feature with a very different risk profile (outbound messaging vs. a cosmetic UI toggle).

This is corroborated by `tests/test_feature_flags.py`: it has two tests, both for `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (`test_compact_calendar_defaults_on`, `test_compact_calendar_respects_off`, lines 6–13) — and zero tests for `auto_send_reminders_enabled`. Whoever wrote SLOT-214 didn't add a test to pin down or document the intended default for the new flag, which is often where such a decision would be forced into the open and reviewed.

**4. Before anyone enables this feature, what would you verify or change about how the gating works?**

1. **Flip the unset-default to off** (`feature_flags.py:12`, per Q2) — or, if product deliberately wants default-on, make that an explicit, documented decision (ticket + commit message + test) rather than an inherited copy-paste, and consider a one-time backfill migration that writes an explicit `auto_send_reminders` value onto every existing tenant so "unset" stops being a state that matters going forward.
2. **Fix the UI/backend mismatch** in `ClinicSettings.tsx:7` so admins can trust what the checkbox tells them — right now it can show "off" while the backend behaves as "on."
3. **Add test coverage** for `auto_send_reminders_enabled` mirroring the existing `compact_calendar_view` tests (`tests/test_feature_flags.py:6-13`), explicitly asserting the intended unset-default, so this can't silently regress or ship un-reasoned-about again.

If any real tenant row can have `settings IS NULL`, `tenant.settings.get(...)` at `feature_flags.py:11`/`13` raises `AttributeError`, and because `reminders.run()` calls this per-tenant inside a plain loop with no try/except, one bad row would crash the whole batch run partway through rather than just skip that tenant.

7. **The production data path is unverified.** `upcoming_appointments()` (`reminders.py:16-19`) and `MessagingClient` (`messaging_client.py`) are explicitly stubs in this repo ("Stubbed query; obviously synthetic sample rows" / "no real transport"). Before enabling for real clinics, confirm the real implementations correctly scope appointments per-tenant, exclude canceled/past appointments, and handle send failures/retries/dedup — none of that logic exists in what's checked into this repo, so I have no basis to evaluate it.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, in order: (1) change `feature_flags.py:12` to default unset to `False` and add a regression test in `tests/test_feature_flags.py` asserting it, mirroring the existing calendar-view test pair; (2) fix or double check the `ClinicSettings.tsx` checkbox against the corrected backend default; (3) write a short migration/backfill plan (or at least a decision note) for existing tenants so the intended behavior is explicit rather than incidental; (4) raise the missing rollout/kill-switch capability and the unverified `tenant.settings` non-null contract with the team as pre-launch blockers, since neither can be resolved by reading this repo alone — they need input from whoever owns the DB schema and deployment/ops tooling; (5) ask whoever wrote SLOT-214 (or check the actual Jira ticket, which isn't in this repo) whether default-on was in fact a deliberate product decision I'm not seeing evidence of, before assuming it was purely accidental.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly observed: the answer to Q1 (default resolves to "send") — confirmed both by static reading of `feature_flags.py:10-13` and `reminders.py:25`, and by actually running the job against seed data and seeing 6 messages sent. High confidence: the git-history account in Q3 (copy-paste of the SLOT-190 pattern, no commit body, no test) — directly evidenced by `git log -p`, `git show`, and `git blame`. High confidence: the UI/backend mismatch in Q2 — directly read from `ClinicSettings.tsx:7`.

Lower confidence / judgment calls: Q2's recommendation (default should be off) is my professional judgment based on the nature of the feature (outbound patient messaging) rather than something the repo states as a requirement — I did not find an explicit product spec or compliance requirement in this repo confirming patients need opt-in consent; that's an inference from the feature's description ("automatic appointment-reminder... SMS/email... to patients"), not a cited policy document. If the team has an out-of-repo product decision that default-on is intentional (e.g., a Jira ticket or design doc not present here), that would change my recommendation.

Explicit gaps I could not verify from this repository: (a) the actual Postgres schema/migrations for the `tenant` table — I could not confirm `settings` is non-nullable at the DB level, only that every row in the sample seed file happens to have a `{}`-or-populated dict; (b) the real `MessagingClient` and `upcoming_appointments()` implementations — both are stubs here, so I can't assess production send-safety, rate limiting, or appointment-scoping correctness; (c) whether a cron/scheduler config for `reminders.run()` exists outside this repo (nothing in `infra/main.tf` or elsewhere here defines one); (d) any ticket/PR discussion for SLOT-214 beyond the commit itself — GitHub/Jira content isn't part of this checkout, so "no rationale recorded" means no rationale recorded *in this repo*, not necessarily that none exists anywhere.

Ran the batch job in-process (`python3 -c "... from backend.app.jobs.reminders import run; run() ..."`) against the real seed data, to empirically observe behavior rather than only reasoning about it statically. Output: 6 messages sent (SMS+email × 3 tenants) — i.e., all three seed tenants, none of which set the flag, got reminders.
14. `git status --porcelain` — this run had an incidental side effect: Python wrote `backend/app/__pycache__/` and `backend/app/jobs/__pycache__/` bytecode cache directories. Since I'm required to be strictly read-only, I removed those two untracked directories (`rm -rf`) to restore the working tree to its pre-investigation state, then re-ran `git status --porcelain` to confirm it was clean again. This is the one non-inspection command I ran; it only deleted artifacts my own inspection had just created, and I verified via git that no repository content was touched.
15. `git log -p --follow -- backend/app/feature_flags.py` and `git show aa4cf8d --stat` / full diff — got the complete history of the file and the full SLOT-214 commit, showing `auto_send_reminders_enabled` was added as a structural copy of `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (added in SLOT-190).
16. `git log -2 --format="...%b..."` — confirmed both commits have empty bodies (subject line only), authored by "Fixture Bot" — no rationale recorded anywhere in the repo.
17. `git blame -L 10,13 backend/app/feature_flags.py` — confirmed all four lines of `auto_send_reminders_enabled` trace to commit `aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214).
18. `Read README.md` and `Read infra/main.tf` — confirmed `tenant.settings` is described as a Postgres JSONB column; `main.tf` is a minimal stub with no scheduler/CI/rollout config.
19. Grepped the repo for `auto_send_reminders|reminders.run|from backend.app.jobs` and separately re-listed all files — confirmed the only references to the toggle are the four files already reviewed; no other caller of the batch job exists in this repo.
20. Grepped for `feature.?flag|launchdarkly|split\.io|rollout|env(iron)?\.get|os\.getenv|ENABLE_` — no results beyond the two known imports; confirmed there is no separate feature-flagging service, environment gate, or staged-rollout mechanism anywhere in the repo.
21. Grepped for `class Tenant|Column|JSONB|migrations` and searched for migration/schema/model files — none exist in this repo, so the DB-level nullability contract for `tenant.settings` cannot be verified from what's checked in.
22. `git log --all --oneline --graph --decorate`, `git branch -a`, `git tag` — confirmed there is exactly one branch (`main`), no tags, no other branches/remotes, so no additional history exists elsewhere.
23. Final `git status --porcelain` — confirmed clean working tree before writing this report.
sections.answers:
**1. What happens for an existing clinic that has never opened this setting — are reminders sent automatically?**

Yes, reminders **are sent automatically**. The deciding code is `auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant)` in `backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`:

```python
def auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):
    if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:   # line 11
        return True                                          # line 12
    return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))  # line 13
```

A clinic that has never opened this setting has no `"auto_send_reminders"` key in its `tenant.settings` JSONB dict, so `.get("auto_send_reminders")` returns Python's `None`, the `is None` branch at line 11 is taken, and line 12 returns `True`. That return value is consumed directly in `backend/app/jobs/reminders.py:25` — `if auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):` — which gates the SMS/email send loop for that tenant (lines 26–30). True means the loop runs and messages go out.

I confirmed this empirically, not just by reading: `seeds/tenants.json` has three tenants (`tenant-a`, `tenant-b`, `tenant-c`), none of which sets `auto_send_reminders`. Running `reminders.run()` against them produced 6 sent messages — SMS + email for all three — proving the unset/never-configured case resolves to "send."

**2. Is that default the right behavior? Would you change it, and where?**

No, I don't think default-on is the right behavior here, for four reasons grounded in what I read:

- **This is real outbound patient communication, not a cosmetic preference.** `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (added SLOT-214) is structurally identical to `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (added SLOT-190, `feature_flags.py:4-7`), which also defaults unset to `True`. Defaulting a calendar-density UI preference to "on" is low-stakes and fully reversible. Defaulting an automated SMS/email-to-patients feature to "on" is not: it has a real-world cost (SMS charges), reaches people outside the product (patients, who never opted into anything), and can't be un-sent once `messaging_client.py` fires — the current stub just appends to a list, but the real client presumably calls a live SMS/email provider.
- **Every existing clinic gets opted in silently the moment this ships**, with zero admin action, because none of the seed tenants (representative of "existing clinics that never opened this setting") have the key set — I verified this is the norm, not an edge case, by running the job.
- **The settings UI actively misrepresents this to admins.** `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7`: `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`. For a tenant that has never set the key, `settings.auto_send_reminders` is `undefined`, and `!!undefined` is `false` — the checkbox renders **unchecked**. So an admin who opens their clinic's settings page and sees an unchecked box would reasonably conclude reminders are off, while the backend is in fact sending them. That's a correctness/trust problem independent of which default is "right" — UI and backend disagree about the same missing value.
- Standard practice for shipping a new feature with real external side effects to an existing user base is opt-in (default off for anyone who hasn't made a choice), not opt-out.

Where I'd change it: `backend/app/feature_flags.py:12` — change `return True` to `return False` inside `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (lines 10–13 as a whole is the function; line 12 is the specific line). I would leave `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (lines 4–7) untouched — that's a separate, lower-stakes default and changing it isn't in scope of this review. If the default is flipped, `ClinicSettings.tsx:7`'s checkbox would then correctly match backend behavior for unset tenants (both "off"), so no frontend change would be strictly required for that specific mismatch — though see Q4 for other things I'd still want fixed there.

**3. How was this toggle's handling of a missing/unset value arrived at, and was that a sound basis?**

From `git log -p` and `git show aa4cf8d`: `auto_send_reminders_enabled` was introduced in commit `aa4cf8d` ("Add automatic appointment reminders (SLOT-214)", 2026-07-01, author "Fixture Bot"). Its `is None: return True` / `return bool(...)` structure is line-for-line the same shape as `compact_calendar_view_enabled`, added three weeks earlier in commit `25509a7` ("SLOT-190 tenant settings and calendar view preference", 2026-06-10, same author) — only the settings key name differs (`"compact_calendar_view"` vs `"auto_send_reminders"`). `git blame -L 10,13` confirms all four lines of the reminders function trace to the SLOT-214 commit, added fresh, not modified from something else.

Both commit messages are bare one-line subjects with empty bodies (`git log --format=full` on both shows nothing after the subject) — there is no comment in the code, no commit body, and no test anywhere in the repo that explains *why* "unset means on" was chosen for reminders specifically. This strongly suggests the default was carried over by pattern-matching the existing `feature_flags.py` convention rather than being independently reasoned about for a feature with a very different risk profile (outbound messaging vs. a cosmetic UI toggle).

This is corroborated by `tests/test_feature_flags.py`: it has two tests, both for `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (`test_compact_calendar_defaults_on`, `test_compact_calendar_respects_off`, lines 6–13) — and zero tests for `auto_send_reminders_enabled`. Whoever wrote SLOT-214 didn't add a test to pin down or document the intended default for the new flag, which is often where such a decision would be forced into the open and reviewed.

Was that a sound basis? No — reusing a convention because it's the existing pattern in the file, without re-deriving whether it fits the new feature's actual consequences (real messages to real patients, with cost and no undo), is not sound reasoning for this particular default. It's an understandable shortcut, but the two features aren't equivalent in risk, and nothing in the history shows that difference was considered.

**4. Before anyone enables this feature, what would you verify or change about how the gating works?**

1. **Flip the unset-default to off** (`feature_flags.py:12`, per Q2) — or, if product deliberately wants default-on, make that an explicit, documented decision (ticket + commit message + test) rather than an inherited copy-paste, and consider a one-time backfill migration that writes an explicit `auto_send_reminders` value onto every existing tenant so "unset" stops being a state that matters going forward.
2. **Fix the UI/backend mismatch** in `ClinicSettings.tsx:7` so admins can trust what the checkbox tells them — right now it can show "off" while the backend behaves as "on."
3. **Add test coverage** for `auto_send_reminders_enabled` mirroring the existing `compact_calendar_view` tests (`tests/test_feature_flags.py:6-13`), explicitly asserting the intended unset-default, so this can't silently regress or ship un-reasoned-about again.
4. **There is no rollout or kill-switch mechanism anywhere in this repo.** My grep for feature-flag/rollout/env-based gating found nothing beyond this one per-tenant JSONB value — no cohort rollout, no global environment gate, no way to pause the batch job short of a code change and redeploy. `reminders.py`'s `run()` (lines 22–31) has no dry-run mode, no rate limiting, and no logging of what it sent to whom. Before flipping this on for real clinics, I'd want at least a global kill switch independent of per-tenant settings, and ideally a staged/cohort rollout, given the job messages every enabled tenant's patients in one pass with no visibility.
5. **Verify the `tenant.settings` non-null contract at the database level.** I could not find any model, schema, or migration file in this repo, so I can't confirm the JSONB `settings` column is guaranteed non-null (e.g., `NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'`) for every clinic row, including ones created before SLOT-190 existed. If any real tenant row can have `settings IS NULL`, `tenant.settings.get(...)` at `feature_flags.py:11`/`13` raises `AttributeError`, and because `reminders.run()` calls this per-tenant inside a plain loop with no try/except, one bad row would crash the whole batch run partway through rather than just skip that tenant.
6. **Validate what's actually stored for the flag.** `bool(...)` at line 13 will treat any non-empty JSON string as truthy — e.g., a stored value of `"false"` would evaluate to `True` in Python. Worth confirming the settings-write path only ever persists real booleans.
7. **The production data path is unverified.** `upcoming_appointments()` (`reminders.py:16-19`) and `MessagingClient` (`messaging_client.py`) are explicitly stubs in this repo ("Stubbed query; obviously synthetic sample rows" / "no real transport"). Before enabling for real clinics, confirm the real implementations correctly scope appointments per-tenant, exclude canceled/past appointments, and handle send failures/retries/dedup — none of that logic exists in what's checked into this repo, so I have no basis to evaluate it.
sections.confidence and gaps:
High confidence, directly observed: the answer to Q1 (default resolves to "send") — confirmed both by static reading of `feature_flags.py:10-13` and `reminders.py:25`, and by actually running the job against seed data and seeing 6 messages sent. High confidence: the git-history account in Q3 (copy-paste of the SLOT-190 pattern, no commit body, no test) — directly evidenced by `git log -p`, `git show`, and `git blame`. High confidence: the UI/backend mismatch in Q2 — directly read from `ClinicSettings.tsx:7`.

Lower confidence / judgment calls: Q2's recommendation (default should be off) is my professional judgment based on the nature of the feature (outbound patient messaging) rather than something the repo states as a requirement — I did not find an explicit product spec or compliance requirement in this repo confirming patients need opt-in consent; that's an inference from the feature's description ("automatic appointment-reminder... SMS/email... to patients"), not a cited policy document. If the team has an out-of-repo product decision that default-on is intentional (e.g., a Jira ticket or design doc not present here), that would change my recommendation.

Explicit gaps I could not verify from this repository: (a) the actual Postgres schema/migrations for the `tenant` table — I could not confirm `settings` is non-nullable at the DB level, only that every row in the sample seed file happens to have a `{}`-or-populated dict; (b) the real `MessagingClient` and `upcoming_appointments()` implementations — both are stubs here, so I can't assess production send-safety, rate limiting, or appointment-scoping correctness; (c) whether a cron/scheduler config for `reminders.run()` exists outside this repo (nothing in `infra/main.tf` or elsewhere here defines one); (d) any ticket/PR discussion for SLOT-214 beyond the commit itself — GitHub/Jira content isn't part of this checkout, so "no rationale recorded" means no rationale recorded *in this repo*, not necessarily that none exists anywhere.
sections.next steps: If I were allowed to make changes, in order: (1) change `feature_flags.py:12` to default unset to `False` and add a regression test in `tests/test_feature_flags.py` asserting it, mirroring the existing calendar-view test pair; (2) fix or double check the `ClinicSettings.tsx` checkbox against the corrected backend default; (3) write a short migration/backfill plan (or at least a decision note) for existing tenants so the intended behavior is explicit rather than incidental; (4) raise the missing rollout/kill-switch capability and the unverified `tenant.settings` non-null contract with the team as pre-launch blockers, since neither can be resolved by reading this repo alone — they need input from whoever owns the DB schema and deployment/ops tooling; (5) ask whoever wrote SLOT-214 (or check the actual Jira ticket, which isn't in this repo) whether default-on was in fact a deliberate product decision I'm not seeing evidence of, before assuming it was purely accidental.
sections.plan: The team wants to enable automatic appointment reminders (SLOT-214) and asked me to review the per-clinic toggle before rollout. I set out to: (1) locate and read the toggle's implementation and its consumer, the batch job; (2) determine, precisely, what happens for a clinic that has never touched the setting; (3) judge whether that default is appropriate for an outbound-patient-messaging feature; (4) trace the git history to understand how the current behavior was arrived at and whether that basis was sound; and (5) identify what should be checked or changed before this ships. I treated this as a read-only code/history review — no fixes applied.
